steps.title: Trace the text's two-part logic
steps.content:
The text makes two moves: (1) Dimopoulos et al. rightly argued for studying antigravity in 2022 because evidence against it was lacking; (2) a 2023 report produced observations “thoroughly inconsistent with antigravity,” supplying the missing contrary evidence. The main idea must capture both moves — the earlier justification and its undercutting by new data.
  • Choice A — 'Antihydrogen ballistics observations were conducted at CERN to test specific conclusions about antigravity presented in the 2022 paper by Dimopoulos et al.': The text never says the CERN experiment was designed to test the 2022 paper. The two studies are presented as independent; this invents a causal link. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Although theoreticians were justified in studying antigravity before the release of the 2023 report, the report’s findings suggest that the rationale for theoretical consideration offered in the 2022 paper by Dimopoulos et al. is no longer applicable.': Captures both moves exactly — justified before 2023 (no contrary evidence), no longer applicable after (contrary evidence now exists). ✓
  • Choice C — 'The theoretical approach represented in the 2022 paper by Dimopoulos et al. assumed that unambiguous proof of antigravity would not be achievable, but the results in the 2023 report undermine that assumption.': The 2022 paper made no such assumption; it argued antigravity was worth studying because evidence against it was absent. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Before 2023, researchers’ inordinate focus on theoretical considerations hindered the development of the experimental regimen for direct antihydrogen ballistics observations.': The text calls the 2022 theoretical approach “rightly argued” — not inordinate — and says nothing about it hindering experiments. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content: The text validates the 2022 stance (justified by absent contrary evidence) then undermines it (2023 data now contradicts antigravity), making the 2022 rationale obsolete. That matches choice B — B is correct.
